sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Lymphoedema screening identifies patients with chronic or progressive swelling who may need compression-focused management, skin care, and specialist review, while ruling out urgent mimics such as DVT, infection, or acute limb threat.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Chronic or progressive limb swelling
- Heaviness, tightness, or reduced endurance
- Skin changes, recurrent cellulitis, or difficulty fitting clothing or devices
- History of cancer treatment, surgery, lymph node removal, or chronic venous issues

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- New unilateral swelling may reflect DVT, acute infection, trauma, or vascular compromise rather than stable lymphoedema
- Skin integrity and infection history are major risk considerations
- Compression is not appropriate until acute limb threats are considered

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen for red, hot, painful swelling, fever, or acute vascular concerns
- Escalate urgent presentations rather than treating them as routine swelling management
- Provide simple protection, elevation, and skin-care advice where appropriate

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Document distribution, chronicity, triggers, skin changes, and function impact
- Refer for specialist lymphoedema assessment, compression planning, and long-term management when the pattern is consistent with chronic lymphatic swelling
- Coordinate oncology, vascular, or medical review when diagnosis is uncertain

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Good lymphoedema screening is as much about ruling out dangerous swelling as it is about identifying chronic fluid overload
